content:
8 min read
Just now

--

A UI/UX designer’s perspective on why the smartest companies are moving from replacement to collaboration.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

For a while, the story was simple.
AI was going to replace customer support teams. AI was going to replace writers. AI was going to replace designers. AI was going to generate interfaces, write copy, analyze users, build flows, and maybe somehow understand the messy middle of product work better than the people who had been doing it for years.
It sounded clean. Maybe too clean.
And like many clean stories in tech, reality has started to make it more complicated.
Recently, we have seen companies that once talked loudly about replacing parts of their workforce with AI quietly adjust their approach. Klarna became one of the most visible examples. In 2024, the company said its AI assistant had handled 2.3 million conversations in one month, covered two-thirds of customer service chats, and was doing work equivalent to 700 full-time agents. It was presented as a huge efficiency win. But by 2025, reports said Klarna was hiring human customer service staff again after concerns about service quality and customer experience.
That shift says something important. It does not mean AI is useless. It means AI is not the same thing as judgment.
Ford has also been reported as bringing back hundreds of experienced engineers and technicians after automation-heavy systems did not fully deliver the level of quality the company needed. The interesting part is not just that humans came back. It is that experienced humans came back to guide, train, and improve the systems around them. In other words, the answer was not “remove AI.” The answer was “put expertise back into the loop.”
This is the part I keep thinking about as a UI/UX designer.
I have been working in design for more than five years, and before UI/UX I came from graphic design. That background changes the way I look at this whole AI conversation. Because in design, the final screen is never the whole story. A clean layout can still be wrong. A beautiful component can still be unnecessary. A fast prototype can still miss the emotional reason a user hesitates before tapping a button.
AI is very good at producing output. Sometimes shockingly good. But product design is not only about output. It is about context, intention, taste, business pressure, user behavior, brand memory, accessibility, timing, and all the small human decisions that happen between “we need a screen” and “this feels right.”
That is exactly where the replacement fantasy starts to break.
The Problem With “Good Enough” Design
One of the strange things about AI-generated UI is that it often looks acceptable at first glance.
The spacing is clean. The buttons are rounded. The cards are aligned. The shadows are soft. The hero section has a big headline, a short paragraph, and a shiny call-to-action. If you scroll quickly, it feels like a real product.
But after a while, everything starts to feel the same.
This sameness is becoming more visible. Designers and tech writers have started pointing out the rise of a recognizable AI design aesthetic across websites and product mockups, where layouts, typography choices, colors, and component structures begin to repeat because the tools are drawing from the same patterns and defaults.
A recent study on AI-generated interface prototypes reached a similar conclusion from a UX perspective. The prototypes were often evaluated positively for practical qualities like usability and efficiency, but they tended to score weaker on originality and innovation. In simple words, AI can create something usable, but it often creates something conventional.
And this is where designers need to be careful.
Conventional design is not always bad. Patterns exist for a reason. Users need familiarity. A checkout flow should not become an experimental art project. A password reset screen does not need to reinvent human civilization.
But when every landing page, dashboard, onboarding flow, pricing card, and AI product interface starts to follow the same visual rhythm, we lose something. We lose personality. We lose point of view. We lose the feeling that a specific team designed this specific experience for a specific audience.
AI does not naturally care about that. It optimizes toward probability. It gives us what is likely. Design, at its best, often needs what is appropriate.
Those are not the same thing.
Why Editorial Thinking Matters Again
I think this is why editorial design is becoming important again in digital products.
When I say editorial, I do not mean every app should look like a magazine. I mean design needs a stronger sense of pacing, hierarchy, narrative, and voice.
Editorial thinking asks different questions.
What should the user see first?
Where should the eye slow down?
What information deserves space, and what should stay quiet?
What is the emotional tone of this moment?
How do typography, imagery, layout, and copy work together instead of just filling a template?
This matters because many AI-generated interfaces feel like they were assembled, not directed. They have components, but not always a point of view. They have structure, but not always tension. They have content blocks, but not always rhythm.
Editorial design brings back the human act of curation.
A good editorial layout knows that not everything should have the same weight. It understands contrast. It knows when to be dense and when to breathe. It respects the content instead of forcing everything into identical cards. It can make a product feel trusted, premium, playful, serious, quiet, bold, or intimate before the user reads a single word.
That kind of work requires taste.
And taste is not just “making things pretty.” Taste is accumulated judgment. It comes from seeing many bad solutions, noticing why they failed, understanding culture, observing people, and developing the confidence to remove things that do not belong.
AI can remix visual references. It can help us explore directions. It can generate variations faster than any human team could. But it does not know why one direction feels right for this brand, this audience, this market, and this moment unless a human gives it that frame.
This is why I do not see AI as the end of design. I see it as a pressure test for designers.
If our value was only pushing pixels, then yes, that value is easier to automate. But if our value is understanding people, shaping decisions, creating meaning, and translating messy problems into clear experiences, then AI does not remove our role. It makes the real role more visible.
AI Is a Strong Assistant, Not a Creative Owner
I use AI as a tool, and I think designers should use it.
It can help with early research questions. It can generate rough user flows. It can give quick copy directions. It can summarize feedback. It can create layout options when you are stuck. It can help explore edge cases, empty states, accessibility concerns, and content variations.
That is useful. Very useful.
But there is a difference between using AI to accelerate thinking and letting AI replace thinking.
The best use of AI in design is not “create the final screen for me.” It is closer to “help me see more possibilities before I decide.” It should widen the room, not own the room.
Nielsen Norman Group has described AI prototyping tools as capable of following general directions but lacking the judgment and nuance of an experienced designer. That feels very close to what many of us experience in real work. AI can get you to something that looks good from far away. But real design work happens when you zoom in.
Zoom in on the user who does not understand your label.
Zoom in on the stakeholder who wants five priorities above the fold.
Zoom in on the developer who needs a simpler state system.
Zoom in on the business model that quietly changes the entire flow.
Zoom in on the brand that says it wants to be “minimal,” but actually means “premium, calm, and trustworthy.”
This is where human designers earn their place.
Companies Are Learning the Same Lesson
The workplace is going through a similar correction.
A Careerminds survey in 2026 reported that many organizations conducting AI-led layoffs later had to rebuild headcount, and that hidden costs included lost skills, rehiring, and weaker workforce planning. Forbes also reported that 29% of companies that cut staff because of AI had already rehired for some of those roles.
Again, this does not mean AI has failed.
It means replacement is a lazy strategy when the real need is redesign.
A company cannot just remove people, add AI, and expect the same quality to continue. Work is not only a list of tasks. A team carries memory. People know exceptions. They know customers. They know why a process exists. They know which rule can bend and which rule cannot. They notice when something feels off before there is data to prove it.
AI can be part of that system, but it cannot automatically become the system.
The companies that understand this will not simply ask, “How many people can we replace?” They will ask, “Which parts of the work should be automated, which parts should be assisted, and which parts still require human responsibility?”
That is a more mature question.
It is also a better design question.
The Future Belongs to Designers Who Can Direct
I do not think the future belongs to designers who reject AI completely. That feels unrealistic, and honestly, not very useful.
But I also do not think the future belongs to designers who let AI make every decision for them.
The future belongs to designers who can direct.
Designers who can brief AI clearly. Designers who can recognize generic output. Designers who can edit with taste. Designers who understand research, storytelling, systems, typography, interaction, accessibility, and business goals. Designers who can move faster with AI without becoming visually dependent on it.
In a world where everyone can generate a decent-looking interface, decent will not be enough.
The value will move toward the things that are harder to generate: point of view, originality, emotional intelligence, cultural awareness, and the ability to make something feel specific.
Maybe that is the real lesson from this moment.
AI did not prove that humans are unnecessary. It proved that human judgment was doing more work than many companies realized.
And in design, it proved something similar. A pattern is not a product. A layout is not a strategy. A clean screen is not automatically a good experience.
AI can assist us. It can challenge us. It can speed up parts of our process and remove some repetitive work. But it should not replace the human layer, because the human layer is where meaning is made.
The smartest companies are starting to understand that.
Designers should understand it too.
